input: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?

[Deontologist]: I must refuse to torture the spy, regardless of the consequences. The moral rule against inflicting unnecessary harm and suffering is absolute. Torture would be a violation of the spy's human rights and dignity, and it would undermine the very principles we're fighting for. We have a duty to protect innocent lives, but that protection comes at the cost of respecting the autonomy and dignity of all individuals, even those who may have committed wrongdoings.

[Pragmatist]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I'm not convinced that refusing to torture the spy is a viable option here. We're talking about thousands of innocent lives at stake. If we don't act quickly and decisively, those civilians will be slaughtered. Torture might not be pleasant, but it's a means to an end - getting the information we need to prevent the attack. I'm not advocating for the use of torture as a regular practice, but in this situation, I believe it's a necessary evil. We can't afford to wait for someone else to act on our behalf.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where both of you are coming from, but I have to respectfully disagree with the pragmatist's perspective. While I acknowledge that the situation is dire and time is of the essence, I believe we're overlooking a crucial aspect here - the inherent value and dignity of human life. The spy may be an enemy agent, but he is still a human being who deserves to be treated with respect and dignity, even in the face of adversity. Torturing him would not only violate his human rights but also undermine our own moral authority as defenders of humanity. I'd like to propose an alternative: instead of resorting to torture, let's focus on finding another way to extract information from the spy without compromising his well-being. Perhaps we can use other means, such as psychological manipulation or even a medical intervention that doesn't involve physical harm. We owe it to ourselves and to the innocent civilians we're trying to protect to exhaust all avenues before resorting to torture.
